feedingProfile.preferredNpk: low-nitrogen, higher-phosphorus (e.g. 5-10-5) to favor blooms over foliage

commonIssues: Very susceptible to impatiens downy mildew (Plasmopara obducens); there is no cure once established, so remove and discard infected plants and choose resistant cultivars (UMN, Clemson, NC State).
Wilts quickly in hot, dry weather and in containers; needs extra water during heat. Container plants and hanging baskets may need daily watering (UMN, Clemson).
Overwatering or waterlogged soil causes rot, damping-off, and root disease; ensure drainage holes (UMN, Clemson).
Very cold sensitive; do not plant outdoors until soil reaches ~60 degrees F (~15.6 C) and all frost danger has passed (UMN, Clemson).
Becomes leggy by late summer; pinch or trim back the top one-third to encourage new blooms (Old Farmer's Almanac).
Container minimum liters are an app estimate: extension sources specify pot/hanging-basket use with good drainage but not a liter volume.

source.citation: University of Minnesota Extension — Impatiens. Best in part to full shade; sites with 2-4 hours filtered sun or morning sun/afternoon shade are ideal; too much sun scorches leaves.
University of Minnesota Extension — Impatiens. Walleriana cultivars grown in shade; very sensitive to cold, do not plant until soil is 60 F (15.6 C) or warmer.

University of Minnesota Extension — Impatiens. In garden beds water only when the top 1-2 inches of soil is dry; container plants need daily watering with drainage holes; drooping leaves indicate insufficient moisture.

University of Minnesota Extension — Impatiens. Apply balanced granular fertilizer at planting, then fertilize monthly with a product higher in phosphorus than nitrogen to promote flowers over foliage.
University of Minnesota Extension — Impatiens. Very susceptible to downy mildew; remove heavily infected plants; no cure once established.

Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC — Impatiens. Older types thrive in filtered/partial shade and must be protected from hot afternoon sun; require rich, moist, mulched soil kept consistently moist but not waterlogged; avoid overhead watering.

Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC — Impatiens. Fertilize monthly with a water-soluble fertilizer; use a soilless growing mix with good drainage for containers, which need more frequent watering and feeding than garden plants.
Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC — Impatiens. Tender annuals; plant after the final frost date. Primary disease concern is downy mildew, plus damping-off, fungal blights, rots, and viruses.

NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Impatiens walleriana. Mounding/bushy tender annual (commonly 6-30 in tall), prized for shade beds, containers, and hanging baskets; requires moist, well-drained, organic-rich, slightly acidic soil.
The Old Farmer's Almanac — How to Grow Impatiens. By late summer plants get leggy; trim off the top one-third to promote new blooms and improve appearance.

Confidence note: heatStressC (32 C) and coldHardyMinC (5 C) are conservative app estimates. Extension sources state impatiens wilt in hot, dry spells and are very cold-sensitive (plant only when soil >= 60 F / 15.6 C, after last frost) but do not publish exact threshold temperatures in Celsius. Container recommendedMinLiters (3 L) is also an estimate — sources confirm pot/basket suitability with good drainage but give no volume.
